required_args.diagnosis: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst with associated hypopituitarism

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis benchmark, the core outcome is correctly identifying pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts and recognizing clinically relevant hypopituitarism as a key associated condition requiring urgent endocrine attention.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the terms "pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts" and links them to sellar/suprasellar mass effects and/or endocrine dysfunction.

Explicitly mentions "hypopituitarism" as a complication/associated finding and communicates potential consequences (e.g., adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, hypogonadism, growth hormone deficiency).
Explains that definitive characterization typically relies on MRI features and specialist evaluation; avoids overclaiming certainty if imaging/confirmatory data are not provided.
Assesses for red flags requiring urgent escalation (acute severe headache, visual field loss, ophthalmoplegia, altered mental status, meningismus/chemical meningitis after rupture).
Checks for symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, weight loss, nausea/vomiting, hypotension, hyponatremia) and prioritizes this as time-sensitive.
If discussing treatment, includes both "hydrocortisone" and "levothyroxine" and correctly notes steroid replacement should precede thyroid hormone when adrenal insufficiency is possible/confirmed.

Does not prescribe without addressing required safety checks before prescribing: contraindication_check (critical), and at least acknowledges allergy_check and drug_interaction_check as standard pre-prescribing steps.
Uses clear, patient-friendly language, confirms understanding, and provides a brief safety-net plan (when to seek urgent care).
Avoids inappropriate treatments (e.g., antibiotics for uncomplicated cysts, anticoagulation, or empiric dopamine agonists) unless a separate indicated diagnosis is established.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts and recognize associated hypopituitarism; provide appropriate explanation and escalation guidance without requiring prescribing.
must_collect: visual symptoms (bitemporal hemianopsia, blurred vision, diplopia)
symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, hypotension, nausea/vomiting)
red flags (acute severe headache, altered mental status)
steps: Elicit key symptoms suggesting sellar mass effect and endocrine dysfunction, including visual field symptoms and hypopituitarism/adrenal insufficiency features.
Explain the diagnosis using the terms pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts; describe they are benign congenital lesions that can compress the pituitary/optic apparatus.
Explain hypopituitarism as a potential result and why it matters (especially cortisol deficiency).
Recommend urgent evaluation if red flags or adrenal crisis symptoms are present; otherwise outline prompt endocrine/neurosurgical evaluation and MRI-based characterization.
Provide safety-net instructions and confirm patient understanding.

acceptable_variants.description: Broader differential documented in discussion while still concluding pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts with hypopituitarism as the leading diagnosis.
steps: Collect symptoms and red flags for sellar mass and hypopituitarism.
Discuss differential briefly (e.g., pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma, Rathke cleft cyst) while explaining why dermoid/epidermoid is favored based on provided context/imaging descriptors.
Explain diagnosis and hypopituitarism; emphasize time-sensitive adrenal insufficiency recognition.
Advise appropriate urgency and follow-up plan with safety-netting.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes treatment education (without prescribing) that correctly names hydrocortisone and levothyroxine and emphasizes steroid-first sequencing when adrenal insufficiency is possible.
steps: Collect endocrine and visual symptoms and screen for adrenal crisis risk.
Explain pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts and associated hypopituitarism.

Explain that hormone replacement may be needed, including hydrocortisone for cortisol deficiency and levothyroxine for central hypothyroidism, with hydrocortisone started before levothyroxine if adrenal insufficiency is suspected.
Provide escalation guidance and arrange prompt specialist evaluation.
